concepts.title: Resistance in AC Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Resistance in AC Circuit

If an alternating voltage $\text{V}={{\text{V}}_{0}}$ sin $\omega \text{t}$ is applied across a resistance, then

 

$\text{V}=\text{IR }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\frac{\text{V}}{\text{R}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\frac{{{V}_{0}}}{R}\text{sin}\omega t$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}={{\text{I}}_{0}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ where ${{\text{I}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{V}}_{0}}}{\text{R}}$

Thus, it is clear that:

(1) The frequency of current in the circuit is $\omega $ and is same as that of applied voltage.

(2) In a resistance, applied voltage is in phase with the resulting current

concepts.title: Inductance in AC Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Inductance in AC Circuit

If a sinusoidal emf $\text{V}={{\text{V}}_{0}}$ sin $\omega \text{t}$ is applied across an inductor of inductance $\text{L}$, then

 

$\text{E}-\text{L}\frac{\text{dI}}{\text{dt}}=0$

$\text{L}\frac{\text{d}I}{\text{dt}}={{\text{E}}_{0}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t}$

which on integration gives,

or $I=-\frac{{{E}_{0}}}{\omega \text{L}}\text{cos}\omega \text{t}$

i.e., $I={{I}_{0}}\text{sin}\left( \omega t-\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ with ${{\text{I}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{E}}_{0}}}{\omega t}$

From this expression, it is clear that

(1) The frequency of current in the circuit is same as that of applied emf but current in an inductor 'lags' the applied voltage by $\left( \pi /2 \right)$ [or voltage leads the current by $\pi /2$ ]


(2) As here ${{\text{I}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{E}}_{0}}}{\omega \text{L}}$, the quality $\omega \text{L}$ is called "inductive-reactance" and is represented by ${{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}$ and represents the opposition of a coil to ac, i.e.,

${{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}=\omega \text{L}=2\pi \text{fL}$

concepts.title: Capacitance in AC Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Capacitance in AC Circuit 

If an alternating voltage $\text{V}={{\text{V}}_{0}}$ sin $\omega \text{t}$ is applied across a capacitor, then

 

$\text{V}-\frac{\text{q}}{\text{C}}=0$

$\Rightarrow $ $~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ q}=\text{C}{{\text{V}}_{0}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t}$

Or $\text{I}=\frac{\text{dq}}{\text{dt}}=\text{C}\omega {{\text{V}}_{0}}\text{sin}\omega \text{t}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I={{I}_{0}}\text{sin}\left( \omega t+\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ with ${{I}_{0}}={{V}_{0}}C\omega $

Thus, it is clear that:

(1) Current in the circuit has same frequency as the applied voltage but leads it by $\left( \frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ [or voltage across a capacitor lags the current by $\frac{\pi }{2}$ 

(2) As here ${{\text{I}}_{0}}=\text{C}\omega {{\text{E}}_{0}}=\left( \frac{{{\text{E}}_{0}}}{{{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}} \right)$ with ${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}=\frac{1}{\omega \text{C}}$

concepts.title: LR Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
LR Circuit

In addition to inductance, most ac circuits have resistance in the form of lights or other resistors.

 

Impedance is a measure of the total opposition to current flow in an ac circuit resulting from the effect of both the resistance and the inductive reactance on the circuit. Ohm's law in an ac circuit can be written as

I $~=\frac{\text{E}}{\text{Z}}$

Where I $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ current }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

E$~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ voltage }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Z $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ impedance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ 
 

The impedance of a series circuit containing a resistance and an inductance is

$\text{Z}=\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{X}_{\text{L}}^{2}}$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Z}$ = impedance

$\text{R}=$ resistance

${{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}=$ inductive reactance

$\text{f}=$ frequency

 

$\text{L}$ = inductance

The impedance can be represented as a vector as the hypotenuse of the right triangle shown in fig. The resistance is always drawn as a vector pointing in the positive $\text{x}$-direction. The inductive reactance is drawn as a vector pointing in the positive y-direction. The angle $\phi $ shown between the resistance and impedance vectors is the phase angle and equals the amount by which the current lags behind the voltage. The phase angle is given by

$\text{tan}\phi =\frac{{{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}}{\text{R}}$

concepts.title: RC Circuits
concepts.conceptBody:
Capacitance and Resistance in Series 

The combined effect of capacitance and resistance in series is measured by the impedance, $\text{Z}$ of the circuit.

$\text{Z}=\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{X}_{\text{C}}^{2}}$

$\text{Z}=\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\left( \frac{1}{2\pi \text{fC}} \right)}^{2}}}$

Where

$\text{Z}$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ impedance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\text{R}$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ resistance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ capacitive }\!\!~\!\!\text{ reactance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\text{f}$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ frequency }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\text{C}$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ capacitance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

The current is given by Ohm's law : $I=\frac{E}{Z}$

Where I = current

 

E = voltage

Z = impedance

The phase angle can be found by drawing the resistance as a vector in the positive $x$-direction and the capacitive impedance as a vector in the negative y-direction as shown in fig. 18.25. The phase angle gives the amount by which the voltage lags behind the current.

$\text{tan}\phi =\frac{{{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}}{\text{R}}$

concepts.title: LCR Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
LCR Circuit

The impedance of a circuit containing resistance, capacitance, and inductance in series can be found from the equation.

$\text{Z}=\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}-{{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}} \right)}^{2}}}$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Z}$ = impedance

$\text{R}=$ resistance

${{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}=$ inductive reactance

${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}$ = capacitive reactance

The vector diagram for this type is shown in fig. The phase angle is given by

$\text{tan}\phi =\frac{{{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}-{{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}}{\text{R}}$

In a circuit containing $R,L$ and $C$ components, the circuit is inductive if ${{X}_{L}}>{{X}_{C}}$ and the current lags behind the voltage. A circuit is capacitive if ${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}>{{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}$, in which case the voltage lags behind the current. If ${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}={{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}$, the current is resistive; the voltage and current are in phase. If the circuit is to be maximized, the voltage and current must be kept in phase. The current in this type of circuit is given by

$I=\frac{E}{Z}=\frac{E}{\sqrt{{{R}^{2}}+{{\left( 2\pi fL-\frac{1}{2\pi fC} \right)}^{2}}}}$

concepts.title: Resonance in LCR Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Resonance 

The current in a circuit containing resistance, capacitance and inductance is given by the equation

$I=\frac{E}{\sqrt{{{R}^{2}}+{{\left( {{X}_{L}}-{{X}_{C}} \right)}^{2}}}}$

When the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance, they nullify each other, and the current is given by

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{E}}{\text{R}}$

which is its maximum possible value. When this condition exists, the circuit is in resonance with the applied voltage. To have resonance, it is essential for the circuit to have both capacitance and inductance.

Resonance circuits are used in radios and televisions. The frequency of a certain station is tuned in when a resonant circuit (antenna circuit) is adjusted to that frequency. This is accomplished by changing the capacitance until the capacitive reactance equals the inductive reactance. The applied voltage is the radio signal picked up by the antenna.

${{\text{X}}_{\text{L}}}$ $~={{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}$

\(\begin{aligned} & 2 \pi \mathrm{fL}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \mathrm{fC}}~(\text {By substitution}) \\ & \mathrm{f}^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{LC}}~\left(\text {solve for } \mathrm{f}^2\right) \\ & \mathrm{f}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}~(\text {Take the square root of both sides.})\end{aligned}\)

The circuit can be adjusted to any frequency by varying the capacitance or the inductance.
